fact: April 18 is exactly 108 days into the year - that's the same number as the stitches on a baseball!
In the Northern Hemisphere, dandelions are typically exploding everywhere right now, turning into those fluffy white puffballs you can blow wishes on.
Around April 18 in northern climates, mornings often feel crisp and fresh because it sits right in that sweet spot where winter's last cold snaps battle with spring's warm sunshine.
Earthworms are usually having their biggest party of the year underground - the soil is finally warm and moist enough for them to wiggle around like crazy.
April 18 falls in Aries season, which means the sun is in the part of the sky that looks like a ram with curly horns.
Tree buds are typically bursting open so fast in mid-April that you can actually watch leaves unfurl if you stare at a branch for about 20 minutes.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, this date marks the heart of autumn when spider webs often get covered in morning dew and look like nature's jewelry.
The sun is climbing higher in the sky every day around April 18, which means shadows at noon are getting shorter and shorter - perfect for playing shadow tag.
Migrating birds are typically flying overhead in huge flocks right now in North America, following invisible highways in the sky that scientists call flyways.
April 18 is roughly when many temperate places hit that magical temperature where you don't need a jacket but you're not sweating either - around 65-70 degrees Fahrenheit.
Grass is usually growing so fast in many places during mid-April that you can almost hear it stretching toward the sun - some types grow up to 6 inches in a single week.
